network_effects.evidence: Membership scale creates a supplier flywheel: 133M cardholders (FY2024) give Costco buying leverage that enables lower prices, attracting more members. This is a one-sided network effect rather than a true two-sided marketplace, warranting partial rather than full credit. Source: Costco 10-K FY2024 (CIK 0000909832), Membership section.

intangible_assets.evidence: Kirkland Signature private label (~30% of sales, growing) commands premium shelf space and member loyalty. Kirkland effectively functions as a brand asset with above-average gross margins relative to Costco's overall thin-margin model. No formal IP moat. Source: Costco 10-K FY2024 — Product mix discussion; web research (Consumer Reports Kirkland quality surveys 2024).

cost_advantage.evidence: Structural cost advantages: (1) SKU discipline — ~4,000 active SKUs vs 30,000+ for traditional grocery, driving higher per-SKU volume and supplier concessions; (2) negative working capital cycle — members pay before goods are sold, suppliers paid ~30 days later; (3) warehouse format strips merchandising costs; (4) membership fee ($65–$130/yr) covers most of operating expenses, allowing near-zero-margin merchandise pricing. Gross margin ~12.7% vs Walmart ~24%, Target ~33% reflects intentional cost pass-through. Source: Costco 10-K FY2024; Walmart 10-K FY2024; Target 10-K FY2024.

switching_costs.evidence: Annual membership fee creates rational inertia: members must justify $65–$130/yr by shopping enough to recoup membership cost, creating a habit loop. US/Canada renewal rate 92.9% (FY2024), worldwide 90.5% — among highest in any subscription retail business. Members who spend >$250/yr (majority) would see negative NPV from cancelling. Costco Executive membership ($130) has >50% of members and auto-renewed at high rate. Source: Costco 10-K FY2024 — Membership section.

efficient_scale.evidence: Warehouse club format has high fixed-cost breakeven but generates strong cash returns once a warehouse reaches maturity (~18 months). New market entry is expensive enough (~$50M per warehouse + land) to deter casual competitors. However, BJ's Wholesale Club and Sam's Club (WMT) operate in the same format, limiting the efficient-scale argument to a shared oligopoly rather than a true natural monopoly. Source: Costco 10-K FY2024 — Properties section; BJ's 10-K FY2024.

citations.claim: Warehouse club competitor landscape: BJ's Wholesale, Sam's Club (WMT subsidiary)
